articles.headline: Novel qTRACE method enables high-throughput single-parasite tracking of Plasmodium falciparum growth and drug effects; finding: up to 50% of viable parasites arise from recovery of artemisinin-induced dormancy, with recovery rates differing between artemisinin-susceptible and -resistant parasites, enabling mechanistic investigation of artemisinin dynamics and recrudescence
articles.title_original: High throughput quantitative tracking of Plasmodium falciparum clonal blood stage parasite growth and applications for antimalarial drug discovery.

articles.headline: Analysis of 107 Plasmodium falciparum isolates from Cambodian military and farmers (2014-2016) using whole-genome sequencing showed high mean IBD sharing (40%), with significantly greater relatedness among Oddar Meanchey than Kratie province isolates (p<2.2e-16). Farmer pairs showed 7.2% greater IBD than mixed-occupation pairs (pd=99.9); highly related clusters (>90% IBD) associated with K13 mutations and PfCRT polymorphisms (p=0.0001), suggesting occupation-specific transmission sources warrant tailored interventions during P. falciparum elimination
articles.title_original: Genetic relatedness of Cambodian Plasmodium falciparum isolates is driven by geography and occupation.

articles.headline: Spatiotemporal analysis of imported malaria cases in Anhui Province (2017–2023) identified persistent hotspots in seven districts, with consistent distribution except 2021 (P<0.05). An ensemble ecological niche model (AUC=0.943, TSS=0.759) predicted An. sinensis distribution driven by vegetation index, nighttime brightness, and precipitation. Central Anhui faces highest re-transmission risk, requiring strengthened surveillance and vector control during mosquito season
articles.title_original: Assessing the risk of malaria re-transmission in Anhui Province, China: an integration of spatiotemporal scan statistics and ecological niche modeling.

articles.headline: Retrospective analysis of 11 pediatric malaria patients (median age 9.5 years, 91% Plasmodium falciparum, 72.7% severe malaria) treated with artesunate showed high neurological sequelae despite guideline-appropriate care: 82% residual neurological symptoms, 44% neurocognitive deficits, 4 EEG abnormalities with 3 developing epilepsies, MRI lesions in 3 patients. Clinical improvement occurred in 77.8% by 12 months, emphasizing need for coordinated follow-up in non-endemic settings
articles.title_original: Malaria in Children is Associated with a High Rate of Neurological Sequelae and Need for Rehabilitative Therapies in Germany.

articles.headline: Narrative review of out-of-pocket (OOP) payments for malaria treatment in developing countries reveals that OOP costs significantly impede timely access, disproportionately affecting low-income and rural populations, increasing morbidity and mortality. Government subsidies, community health insurance, and donor-funded programs reduce these barriers. Scaling subsidized care and strengthening public health systems are critical for equitable malaria control
articles.title_original: Implications of out-of-pocket payments for the control of malaria in developing countries: A narrative review.

articles.headline: Case-control study of 144 Ghanaian participants (40 diabetes, 25 diabetes+malaria, 41 malaria-only, 38 controls) demonstrated that malaria-diabetes comorbidity correlates with significantly higher parasitaemia (1,702 vs. 932 parasites, p=0.02) and elevated IgG responses to GLURP-R2 (1.60-fold, β=0.47, p=0.01), GLURP-RO (1.43-fold, β=0.36, p=0.03), and MSP1 (1.42-fold, β=0.35, p<0.05), suggesting diabetes enhances parasite multiplication and antibody responses relevant to vaccine development
articles.title_original: Malaria-diabetes comorbidity is linked to higher parasitaemia and enhanced IgG response to malaria vaccine candidate antigens.

articles.headline: Seasonal mass drug administration with artemether-lumefantrine in Burkina Faso school-aged children showed 93% of initially uninfected children remained infection-free and 91% of treated parasitemic children remained negative during follow-up, with persistent infections stable at 5–9%. Low frequencies of pfcrt/pfmdr1 resistance mutations and identical msp1/msp2 haplotypes over time indicate dry-season transmission interruption and MDA's potential to disrupt dynamics
articles.title_original: Impacts of Seasonal Mass Drug Administration in School-Aged Children on Chronic Malaria Infections in Burkina Faso.

articles.headline: Cross-sectional survey of 372 Nigerian caregivers of under-five children revealed low R21/MATRIX malaria vaccine awareness (9.7%) despite high positive perception (98.1% believe safe/effective), high acceptability (76.6% would 'definitely' vaccinate), and strong willingness to pay (71.2%, with 51.9% willing ₦1,001–₦5,000 per dose). Targeted awareness campaigns are essential to bridge knowledge gaps in endemic communities
articles.title_original: R21/MATRIX malaria vaccine: awareness, perception, acceptability, and willingness to pay among caregivers of under-five children in endemic communities in Lagos, Nigeria.

articles.headline: Cross-sectional assessment of malaria service readiness among 1,156 Ethiopian health facilities providing antenatal care found only 15.7% (95% CI 13.8–18.0) fully ready, with 14.3% lacking key components and substantial regional variation. Governance-related factors predicted readiness: client suggestion boxes (AOR=1.60), recent report submission (AOR=2.79), and monthly staff meetings (AOR=1.64), indicating that supply-chain, workforce, and governance strengthening are essential
articles.title_original: Malaria service readiness and associated factors among health facilities that provide antenatal care services in Ethiopia: a cross-sectional study using generalised estimating equation analysis.

articles.headline: Community-based cross-sectional survey of 912 residents across three Ethiopian towns (January–March 2020) found low malaria prevalence by RDT (0.78%) and qPCR (4%, with 1.7% P. falciparum and 2.3% P. vivax) but higher seroprevalence (19.4% P. falciparum, 18% P. vivax), varying by town. Awash Sebat Kilo showed highest recent exposure (36.5% and 28%, respectively) versus Adama (2.8% both); proximity to breeding sites (OR=2.8, p=0.037) and age >15 years (OR=4.0, p<0.001) were significant risk factors, highlighting marked heterogeneity requiring tailored interventions
articles.title_original: Heterogeneity of malaria transmission in urban settings in Ethiopia: A seroprevalence and risk factor analysis.

articles.headline: Ecological study integrating causal inference and machine learning across 969 Colombian municipalities (2007–2023) analyzing 1,075,112 malaria cases identified non-linear temperature-rainfall-incidence relationships: optimal temperature 25 °C and ~37 mm rainfall for highest Standardized Incidence Ratio, with E-values 1.14–1.48 indicating low-to-moderate unmeasured confounding tolerance. Findings support incorporating rainfall and temperature thresholds into early-warning systems
articles.title_original: Integrating causal inference and machine learning to quantify climate-malaria relationships: Evidence of temperature and rainfall thresholds from Colombian municipalities.

articles.headline: Qualitative study of 39 Greater Mekong Subregion stakeholders identified that successful malaria elimination requires strengthening all six WHO health system building blocks at national level, including strong political commitment, targeted high-risk interventions, and reliable supply chains. Major barriers include lack of elimination policies for high-risk groups, incomplete private-sector reporting, workforce gaps, supply-chain constraints, and declining international donor funding, necessitating comprehensive health-system assessments for readiness
articles.title_original: Strengthening the health systems at national level for malaria elimination in the Greater Mekong Subregion countries: a qualitative study.

articles.headline: Analysis of 950 Plasmodium vivax CyRPA sequences (including 42 new Colombian isolates) revealed high nucleotide diversity shaped by natural selection and recombination. In-silico epitope mapping identified B- and T-cell epitopes in polymorphic regions with strong predicted HLA binding; immune simulation of a multiepitope C-terminal construct predicted robust humoral memory, establishing the conserved C-terminal region as a promising, broadly reactive vaccine candidate warranting validation
articles.title_original: Genetic diversity, natural selection, and immunological features of the Plasmodium vivax CyRPA protein: Implications for vaccine development.

articles.headline: This review consolidates molecular mechanisms regulating Plasmodium falciparum gametocytogenesis, including the AP2-G transcription factor, epigenetic mechanisms (H3K9me3), heterochromatin proteins, and post-transcriptional regulation via RNA-binding proteins and epitranscriptomic modifications, to identify therapeutic targets and transmission-blocking vaccines
articles.title_original: Molecular mechanisms involved in Plasmodium gametocytogenesis.

articles.headline: Cross-sectional study of 1,618 participants in 35 reactive case detection activities in Unguja island (May 2022–January 2023) found 35.3% exposure to any malaria species, predominantly P. falciparum (29.8%), with lower seroprevalences for P. ovale (5.8%), P. malariae (5.9%), and P. vivax (5.9%), and active infections in 6.0% mostly P. falciparum (4.6%); travel to mainland Tanzania was the dominant risk factor, suggesting current control efforts focused on P. falciparum may inadequately address non-falciparum transmission
articles.title_original: Understanding the Epidemiology of Malaria in Zanzibar Through Molecular and Serological Analysis of Samples Collected During Reactive Case Detection.

articles.headline: In Anopheles gambiae, the proline-specific transporter PfApiAT2 is essential for early Plasmodium falciparum oocyst development; PfApiAT2-knockout parasites showed complete block in sporogony with degenerate oocysts and severely defective sporozoite production, a phenotype rescued by nutrient supplementation to the mosquito, unveiling a critical nutritional dependency and transmission vulnerability
articles.title_original: PfApiAT2 is a proline transporter essential for the transmission of Plasmodium falciparum by the mosquito vector.

articles.headline: Field study of 1,040 An. gambiae complex mosquitoes from 60 villages in southern Benin found Microsporidia MB infection in 1.6% of An. coluzzii and 2.2% of gambiae s.s., with no P. falciparum in Microsporidia-infected mosquitoes. The L1014F kdr mutation was absent in kdr-SS but present in kdr-RS (1.9%) and kdr-RR genotypes, with mean Microsporidia MB DNA load higher in kdr-RR (23.23 ng/µl) versus kdr-RS (10.13 ng/µl), indicating the L1014F kdr allele associates with higher Microsporidia MB prevalence and load
articles.title_original: The L1014F Kdr mutation is associated with a higher prevalence and load of the Plasmodium-blocking symbiont Microsporidia MB In Anopheles Gambiae s.l. In Benin.

articles.headline: This review comprehensively covers malaria's global epidemiology, transmission dynamics, and control strategies, noting that the WHO African Region achieved a 40% reduction in incidence and 60% decline in mortality from 2000–2022 through LLINs, indoor residual spraying, RDTs, intermittent preventive treatment, and artemisinin-based combination therapies, while highlighting emerging threats including insecticide and antimalarial drug resistance and discussing strategies such as seasonal malaria chemoprevention and vaccines RTS,S/AS01 and R21/Matrix-M
articles.title_original: Malaria: a review on its current epidemiological status and management strategies.

articles.headline: Molecular and field study demonstrated that the E205D point mutation in CYP6P3 drives pyrethroid insecticide resistance in An. gambiae; in vitro assays showed the 205D variant had 3.7-fold higher catalytic efficiency for permethrin than the E205 variant, and field data confirmed CYP6P3-E205D correlates with pyrethroid resistance (OR: 26.4; P<0.0001) with the homozygous resistance genotype showing 6.1-fold higher survival with PermaNet 3.0 bed nets versus susceptible (P=0.011)
articles.title_original: The E205D mutation in CYP6P3 drives pyrethroid insecticide resistance in the African malaria mosquito vector Anopheles gambiae.

articles.headline: Study protocol for a community-based cross-sectional survey of approximately 2,000 individuals aged >1 year in Indonesia's new capital Ibu Kota Nusantara in East Kalimantan to assess serological exposure to malaria species (including Plasmodium knowlesi) and dengue virus serotypes using multiplex bead-based assays and malaria RDTs, with statistical and geostatistical models to evaluate seroprevalence, spatial patterns, and risk factors in a region facing increased vector-borne disease transmission
articles.title_original: A study protocol for a baseline community-based cross-sectional study to investigate malaria and dengue transmission dynamics in a development area of new capital city, Indonesia.

articles.headline: Case report of a 46-year-old woman from India with P. vivax malaria presenting with severe anemia, thrombocytopenia, and pulmonary involvement (parasitemia 50%, parasite density 8,645/µL) requiring ICU admission and high-flow oxygen, successfully treated with artemether-lumefantrine and doxycycline with parasitemia clearance by day 3, underscoring the need for clinical suspicion for severe P. vivax in patients with relevant epidemiological exposure
articles.title_original: Severe Malaria Due to Plasmodium vivax With Pulmonary Involvement: A Case Report.

articles.headline: Population genetic analysis of 336 P. vivax isolates genotyped across serial surveys over a decade in East Sepik (2005, 2012, 2016) and Madang Province (2006, 2010, 2014) in Papua New Guinea revealed contrasting spatiotemporal patterns following LLIN distribution and initial transmission decline followed by resurgence: Madang showed population bottleneck at low transmission with rapid recovery during resurgence, while East Sepik showed less dramatic impact and ongoing transmission of multiple residual lineages, demonstrating that sustained long-term control efforts are essential
articles.title_original: Population genetics of Plasmodium vivax with transmission decline and rebound in two endemic areas of Papua New Guinea.

articles.headline: In 6 rhesus macaques co-infected with SIVmac239 (ART started week 8) and P. fragile (week 12), significant CCR5+ CD4+ T cell decline occurred in peripheral blood, colon, and duodenum. Duodenum NET-forming granulocyte frequencies significantly correlated with peripheral SIV burden after P. fragile co-infection but not with parasitemia or CD4+ frequencies, suggesting P. fragile co-infection during ART-treated SIV causes mucosal disruptions contributing to SIV replication despite antiretroviral therapy
articles.title_original: Gastrointestinal Mucosal Disruptions During ART-Treated SIV/Plasmodium fragile Co-Infection.

articles.headline: Immunoprecipitation-mass spectrometry of transgenic P. berghei expressing GFP-fused PbSR1 identified nuclear interactions with RNA-binding proteins and small nuclear ribonucleoproteins, and cytoplasmic interactions with rhoptry proteins RhopH1, RhopH2, and RhopH3. Live-cell fluorescence imaging showed PbSR1-RhopH2 co-localization in trophozoites, immature schizonts, and gametocytes but not mature schizonts or merozoites, indicating these developmental stages are candidates for SR1-RhopH2 cytoplasmic interaction
articles.title_original: Identification of serine/arginine-rich splicing factor 1-interacting proteins in Plasmodium berghei.

articles.headline: Study protocol for a cluster-randomized, double-blinded, placebo-controlled trial of 56 clusters (1:1 intervention:control) to evaluate protective efficacy of a transfluthrin-based spatial repellent device (Mossie-GO™) in reducing malaria prevalence in children ≤5 years in Uganda, with 100 baseline and 60 children per cluster sampled at 6 and 12 months, and secondary endpoints including entomological correlates, user acceptance, and airborne transfluthrin concentration
articles.title_original: Evaluation of the protective efficacy of a transfluthrin-based spatial repellent product to reduce malaria prevalence in Uganda: study protocol for a cluster-randomised double-blinded control trial-the Mossie-GO trial.

articles.headline: SIMPLseq is a 6-locus P. falciparum AmpSeq miniplex with well-specific inline barcode contamination detection. It achieved 100% average locus detection at ≥0.5 parasites/μl with zero false-positive haplotypes and detected all 24 deliberately introduced contaminations plus 39 unintentional contaminations in a 1,420-sample run, outperforming the 4CAST miniplex. The open-source protocol enables high-sensitivity genotyping for recurrent infection classification and polyclonality estimation without costly pre-amplification
articles.title_original: SIMPLseq: a high-sensitivity Plasmodium falciparum genotyping and PCR contamination tracking tool.

articles.headline: October–December 2022 entomological survey across 67 Burkina Faso sites collected 617 An. funestus group members, of which 95% (589/617) were An. funestus s.s.; 3.57% were hybrids between An. rivulorum and An. rivulorum-like. P. falciparum infection was detected in 5.76% (33/572) of An. funestus s.l., with 5.6% in An. funestus s.s. and 0.174% in An. rivulorum-like; 64.3% showed zoophilic behavior. Results update vector distribution and emphasize incorporating An. funestus into vector control interventions
articles.title_original: Updating Anopheles funestus group members in Burkina Faso: species distribution and contribution to malaria transmission.

articles.headline: Six clinically-approved neuroprotective drugs were screened in vitro against P. falciparum and in vivo in P. berghei ANKA-infected C57BL/6 mice with experimental cerebral malaria. Early oral cilostazol, piracetam, or rivastigmine delayed CM onset and improved survival; rivastigmine plus α/β-arteether initiated after first neurological signs significantly improved parasite clearance and survival versus either drug alone. This combination may represent a practical, low-cost adjunct therapy for cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: Evaluation of clinically approved neuroprotective drugs as adjunct therapies in experimental cerebral malaria.

articles.headline: Prospective cohort study of 600 Ugandan children (6 months–4 years) evaluated 12 months post-discharge for cognitive outcomes after severe malaria. Cerebral malaria (CM) and severe malarial anemia (SMA) showed significantly lower cognition z-scores (CM: –0.66, P=0.001; SMA: –0.71, P<0.001) and higher cognitive impairment prevalence (CM 10.6%, SMA 12.1% vs. 1.9% controls). Other severe malaria forms (respiratory distress, multiple seizures, prostration) did not impair long-term cognition, indicating CM and SMA specifically cause persistent cognitive sequelae
articles.title_original: Cerebral Malaria and Severe Malarial Anemia, but not Other Forms of Severe Malaria, Are Associated With Long-Term Cognitive Impairment.

articles.headline: Commentary examining whether all forms of severe malaria produce long-term cognitive impairment, raising questions about mechanistic characterization of cognitive outcomes across different severe malaria phenotypes
articles.title_original: Not All Forms of Severe Malaria Impair Long-term Cognition-More Characterization, More Questions.

articles.headline: Qualitative study of malaria patients at Mhinga Village, Vhembe District, South Africa used semi-structured interviews to explore experiences of signs and symptoms. Thematic analysis identified varied presentations across body systems and circumstances prompting immediate consultation. Study emphasizes awareness of malaria prevention strategies including mosquito avoidance and antimalarial use to reduce transmission
articles.title_original: Experiences of people diagnosed with malaria regarding signs and symptoms at a selected village in the Vhembe District, Limpopo province, South Africa.
articles.authors: Kwinda W, Mudzweda AD, Luhalima T

articles.headline: Analysis of 2019 baseline survey data from three southeastern coastal Tanzanian districts using Slope Index of Inequality and Relative Index of Inequality. Higher socioeconomic status was associated with lower malaria infection risk (OR=0.9975, 95% CI: 0.9972–0.9978), reduced treatment costs (coefficient=–3.13, P<0.05), and fewer workdays lost (coefficient=–0.0017, P<0.05); Rufiji showed the most significant socioeconomic equity. Results underscore need for equity-focused interventions targeting vulnerable populations
articles.title_original: Socioeconomic health equity with malaria burden: quantifying slope (Relative) index of inequity in malaria infection outcomes in the Southeastern-coastal Tanzania.

articles.headline: Retrospective review of 190 Malawian children with cerebral malaria (2013–2019) found cortical edema in 163 (86%), with 63.2% showing diffuse cortical involvement with occipital and peri-Rolandic sparing that inversely correlated with brain volume (β=–0.26, P<0.001) and poor outcome risk (OR: 0.3; 95% CI: 0.1–0.6; P=0.002). Pattern of cortical edema sparing reflects disease severity, becoming progressively involved in more severe cases
articles.title_original: Peri-Rolandic and Occipital Sparing Cortical Edema: A Prevalent MRI Finding in Pediatric Patients with Cerebral Malaria.

articles.headline: Deterministic compartmental model using routine malaria data (January 2015–May 2024) from Kebbi State, Nigeria assessed long-lasting insecticidal net (LLIN) impact. Modeling showed that 80% sustained LLIN usage since 2015 would have averted 5.2 million cases (97.98% reduction); achieving and sustaining 80% usage going forward would avert ~3 million cases by May 2029 (78.93% reduction). Results emphasize scaling LLIN distribution to significantly reduce incidence
articles.title_original: Modelling the effect of long-lasting insecticidal nets on malaria transmission dynamics in Kebbi State, Nigeria.

articles.headline: Deep amplicon sequencing of 89 P. falciparum isolates from high-transmission Kédougou, Senegal identified non-synonymous SNPs in 64 samples (71.9%), yielding 26 distinct SNPs including 15 novel variants; structural modeling predicted 7 of 16 SNPs destabilize PfRIPR, while 3 SNPs (Q737K, T738K, V840L) located in EGF5-8 neutralizing antibody-binding domains. High genetic diversity in this Phase 1a vaccine candidate warrants monitoring for vaccine efficacy impact
articles.title_original: Genetic diversity of the malaria vaccine candidate PfRIPR in a high transmission region of Senegal.

articles.headline: Flow cytometry and microscopy demonstrated P. falciparum's consistent in vitro preference for Duffy-positive RBCs; evolutionary-epidemiological modeling incorporating immunity showed P. falciparum-mediated invasion inhibition increases FYBES (Duffy-negative) allele frequency at low transmission but decreases it at high transmission. Findings suggest DARC involvement in P. falciparum invasion mechanisms and, together with P. vivax selection pressure, help explain Duffy negativity distribution in sub-Saharan Africa
articles.title_original: Invasion preferences suggest a possible role for Plasmodium falciparum parasites in the expansion of Duffy negativity in West and Central Africa.

articles.headline: Analysis of IHME estimates across Nigeria's 37 subnational units (2010–2019) found national malaria incidence in children 0–4 years declined from 103 to 74.5 per 1,000 (27.7% reduction) and mortality from 477.3 to 237.6 per 100,000 (50.2% reduction). Northern states (Zamfara, Kano, Katsina) remained most burdened despite reductions, while southern states (Lagos, Delta, Anambra) consistently recorded lower burden; incidence coefficient of variation fell from 46.3% (2013) to 28.1% (2019). Persistent regional disparities necessitate geographically targeted interventions
articles.title_original: Spatial and temporal inequalities in malaria incidence and mortality among children aged 0-4 years in Nigeria: a subnational analysis, 2010-2019.

articles.headline: Qualitative evaluation of a 4-month faculty enrichment program in applied malaria modeling at Northwestern University (2022-2023). Participants valued technical expertise and communication skills development, with identified challenges in cross-disciplinary learning and adapting to new teaching styles. Instructors adapted program structures in response to feedback, strengthening their own technical capacity. Multi-pronged training approaches with continuous feedback mechanisms enhance long-term value and knowledge transfer at home institutions
articles.title_original: Building contextually-relevant training programs for scientific development: process and lessons learned in implementing two iterations of a faculty enrichment program in applied malaria modeling.

articles.headline: Meta-analysis of 21 studies found overall G6PD deficiency prevalence of 6% (95% CI 0.04–0.08) among malaria patients, ranging from 1% in endemic regions to 23% (95% CI 0.06–0.41) in Jimma. A376G, A376T, and G202A mutant variants dominated, with regional variation in emerging variants (ChrX:154535443, ChrX:6369, ChrX:6504). Authors conclude G6PD deficiency is common in malaria-endemic areas and should inform treatment decisions, particularly for antimalarial drugs with oxidative properties
articles.title_original: Pooled prevalence of Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency among malaria patients in Ethiopia: a systematic review and meta-analysis.

articles.headline: Cross-sectional analysis of 3,255 households in Ghana's 2022 DHS found malaria prevalence of 3.7% in children aged 6–59 months. Unexpected finding: children not sleeping under ITNs had lower odds of malaria (aOR=0.52, 95% CI 0.30–0.92). Anaemia more than doubled malaria odds (aOR=2.03, 95% CI 1.36–3.04); outside toilets strongly increased risk (aOR=16.64, 95% CI 2.06–134.57). Wealth and older household heads protective. Authors recommend targeted interventions in poor households and improvements to sanitation
articles.title_original: Household characteristics, water, sanitation and hygiene (WASH) and malaria prevalence among children aged 6-59 months in Ghana: an analysis of the 2022 Ghana Demographic and Health Survey.

articles.headline: Qualitative study (64 interviews, 16 health facilities in DRC) found healthcare providers significantly mistrusted negative malaria RDT results and overreported cases compared to automated reader data. Providers equated fever with malaria and suspected false negatives due to early infection, improper prior treatment, and RDT limitations. Financial incentives favored microscopy over RDTs. Authors recommend sharing country-level RDT efficacy data and improving provider understanding of test performance to increase guideline adherence
articles.title_original: Positive tests are all alike, every negative test is negative in its own way: lack of confidence in negative malaria rapid diagnostic tests in the Democratic Republic of the Congo.

articles.headline: Case report examining the relationship between post-malaria neurological syndrome and acute disseminated encephalomyelitis in a single patient, highlighting ongoing clinical and diagnostic debate regarding these neurological complications
articles.title_original: Post malaria neurological syndrome and acute disseminated encephalomyelitis: a case highlighting an ongoing debate.

articles.headline: Live-imaging study of P. falciparum tracked apicoplast morphology through the 48-h cell cycle, identifying four distinct developmental stages and a critical "Crown" morphology required for nucleus-apicoplast attachment. Nuclear DNA replication was required for early apicoplast biogenesis; conversely, blocking apicoplast DNA replication spared organelle development but prevented proper segregation. Authors establish a central pathway linking apicoplast development to the cell cycle and an independent mechanism governing organelle inheritance
articles.title_original: Independent nuclear and organellar mechanisms determine apicoplast fate in malaria parasites.

articles.headline: National surveillance analysis (2010–2024) of 52,627 malaria cases in Saudi Arabia found marked interannual variability and an increase after 2020, with burden shifting from 7 endemic regions to Aseer and Jazan. P. falciparum and P. vivax/P. ovale predominated in individuals ≥10 years; from 2021 onward, no indigenous transmission occurred and most cases were imported. ARIMA forecasting projects stable trends through 2030. Authors emphasize sustained surveillance and prevention of re-establishment of local transmission
articles.title_original: Malaria: Examining Persistence at the Margins of Endemicity over 15 Years in Saudi Arabia.

articles.headline: Outbreak investigation identified 4 autochthonous P. falciparum cases (1 fatal) near Benina International Airport, Benghazi, Libya in November 2024. Epidemiologic and entomologic investigation excluded local transmission, confirming airport malaria. Findings underscore vulnerability of malaria-free regions to imported vectors via international travel
articles.title_original: Airport Malaria Cluster in Certified Malaria-Free Country, Libya, 2024.

articles.headline: Spatial-temporal modeling study integrated MaxEnt species distribution modeling for Anopheles vector habitat suitability with fuzzy logic biting probability models, incorporating bed net usage and insecticide resistance data from 2000–2018. Mean validation accuracy was 91% (25 An. gambiae biting observations, 2017–2021). Reduced biting probability with increased bed net usage correlated with declining P. falciparum prevalence. Authors conclude integrating vector presence, climate, human availability, and interventions provides an effective early-warning system for malaria elimination strategies
articles.title_original: Spatial-temporal coupling of malaria vector habitat suitability and biting probability.

articles.headline: Narrative review of diabetes-malaria co-morbidity mechanisms in sub-Saharan Africa, identifying malaria-driven inflammation and oxidative stress as disrupting insulin signaling and beta-cell function, while chronic hyperglycemia impairs immune defenses against parasites. TNF-α and insulin signaling pathways emerge as pivotal links orchestrating bidirectional pathogenesis. Authors note higher subclinical infection and prolonged parasitemia in diabetic patients and propose exploiting this relationship for novel therapeutic strategies targeting both diseases
articles.title_original: Diabetes Mellitus and Malaria Co-Existence as Deadly Burden in Sub-Saharan Africa: A Review of Clinical Outcomes and Molecular Interactions.

articles.headline: In vitro study evaluated benzoxaborole compounds as inhibitors of P. falciparum carbonic anhydrase (PfaCA). Compounds 13 and 14 were most potent PfaCA inhibitors (KI=0.45 and 0.43 µM, respectively) with notable antiplasmodial activity (IC50=34 and 47 µM); ethyl ester 7 showed strongest antiplasmodial activity (IC50=2.5 µM). Molecular docking confirmed favorable binding to PfaCA active site. Authors conclude benzoxaborole scaffolds represent a promising new chemotype for antimalarial drug development
articles.title_original: Benzoxaboroles Are Inhibitors of the η-Class Carbonic Anhydrase From Plasmodium falciparum.

articles.headline: Observational study of ~350,000-population Karen State, Myanmar (2020–2024) documented 12-fold increase in P. falciparum and threefold increase in P. vivax cases after COVID-19 pandemic and military coup disrupted diagnosis and treatment services. Increased transmission: P. falciparum (RR=1.72, 95% CI 1.68–1.76) and P. vivax (RR=1.82, 95% CI 1.80–1.84). Malaria deaths remained low; cases declined by 2024. Authors attribute resurgence to service disruption and insecurity, but conclude malaria control systems contained the crisis despite continued uncertainty
articles.title_original: Re-emergence of malaria in Karen State, Myanmar: has the battle of Burma been lost?

articles.headline: Article titled 'Malaria' with no abstract; unable to extract specific study design, population, or quantitative findings
articles.title_original: Malaria.

articles.headline: Whole sporozoite vaccines (WSVs) show sharply reduced efficacy in field trials compared to homologous CHMI studies. Authors hypothesize antigen mismatch (limited parasite genetic diversity) and host-context factors (genetics, infection history, microbiome, nutrition) explain this decline, proposing future vaccines integrate parasite genomics, systems immunology, and endemic-context animal models
articles.title_original: The endemic dilemma: why whole sporozoite malaria vaccine efficacy falls in the field.

articles.headline: Retrospective multicentre analysis of 28 P. ovale wallikeri/P. falciparum and 11 P. ovale curtisi/P. falciparum co-infections versus 117 P. falciparum mono-infections found no differences in clinical severity or hospitalization. P. ovale wallikeri co-infections showed higher leukocytes (6.25 vs 4.85×10⁹/L, p=0.010) and lower parasitemia (0.6% vs 1.0%, p=0.025); P. ovale curtisi co-infections showed lower ALT (18.0 vs 27.3 IU/L, p=0.022) and more thrombocytopenia (100% vs 51%, p=0.003), suggesting negative P. falciparum–P. ovale curtisi interaction
articles.title_original: Comparison of routine clinical profiles of patients with imported P. falciparum malaria and co-infection with Plasmodium ovale wallikeri or Plasmodium ovale curtisi.

articles.headline: Pooled analysis of 102 malaria-naive CHMI control participants (48 female, 54 male) across 13 studies found males had delayed blood-stage P. falciparum detection: mean time-to-positivity 7.59 days versus 7.17 days in females; adjusted TTP 8% later in males (time ratio 1.08, 95% CI 1.03–1.16), with 5.2-fold higher adjusted odds of detection after day 7 (95% CI 1.52–17.70). Authors conclude P. falciparum involves sex-specific liver-stage dynamics
articles.title_original: Sex-based differences in Plasmodium infection in the control groups of controlled human malaria infection trials in malaria-naive populations in the USA and the Netherlands: a pooled analysis.

articles.headline: Thermal profiling method MAP-X combined with machine learning mapped dynamic protein complexes in P. falciparum intact cells, revealing novel complexes and moonlighting proteins with temporal dynamics, shifting understanding from static interactome lists to dynamic temporal networks
articles.title_original: MAP-X unveils the shapeshifting interactome of Plasmodium falciparum.

articles.headline: Commercial qPCR assay for P. vivax in 167 samples showed greater sensitivity than microscopy, detecting 3 additional positives; demonstrated high specificity with no cross-reactivity, supporting its utility as reliable diagnostic tool for malaria detection and surveillance
articles.title_original: Promising diagnostic performance of a commercial qPCR assay for Plasmodium vivax detection: Contributing to malaria control and elimination.

articles.headline: Cross-sectional study of 2,500 suspected malaria patients in Peshawar, Pakistan found PCR detected significantly more cases than microscopy (p=0.001); microscopy accuracy 86.4%, κ=0.692, AUC=0.823. Prevalence peaked September with higher rates in males aged 21–30 (p=0.04). Malaria-positive individuals showed reduced hemoglobin, hematocrit, RBC indices, and elevated liver enzymes; parasite density negatively correlated with hemoglobin/hematocrit/platelets and positively with liver enzymes/bilirubin
articles.title_original: Integrated diagnostic and host biomarker assessment of plasmodium infections in malaria-endemic Peshawar, Pakistan.

articles.headline: Article titled 'Beta-thalassemia and a missing malaria landscape' with no abstract; unable to extract specific study findings or quantitative results
articles.title_original: Beta-thalassemia and a missing malaria landscape.
articles.authors: Elmakhzen B, El Makhzen N, Abriel H

articles.headline: Review of Wolbachia-based malaria vector control via transinfection in Anopheles mosquitoes. Demonstrates Wolbachia-induced Plasmodium suppression is feasible based on dengue control success and Anopheles parasitemia challenge studies. Addresses suppression mechanisms and challenges for translating proof-of-concept approaches to field application
articles.title_original: Wolbachia for malaria control.

articles.headline: Randomized pilot study of 40 houses (4 arms: cool-roof, cross-ventilation, mat-ceiling, control) found cool-roof housing reduced heat index by 3.3°C daytime (p<0.001) and 2.4°C nighttime (p<0.01). Screening reduced female Anopheles funestus by 77% and Culex by 58%; 85% of households expressed willingness to adopt. Cool-roofs combined with vector proofing is effective for heat adaptation and malaria mosquito control
articles.title_original: Housing modifications for heat adaptation, thermal comfort and malaria vector control in rural African settlements.

articles.headline: MSRP1 identified as highly specific marker for sexual commitment in P. falciparum. FACS-based isolation of sexually versus asexually committed parasites enabled proteomics revealing distinct protein signatures, including merozoite surface proteins, providing tools for characterizing gametocyte commitment at this critical life-cycle juncture
articles.title_original: Defining the Proteome of Sexually Committed Parasites in Plasmodium falciparum.

articles.headline: Case report of delayed hemolytic anemia (DHA) 8 days after artemether-lumefantrine in 50-year-old with severe P. falciparum (parasitemia >10%), prior rituximab treatment, and hepatosplenomegaly. Laboratory findings included reticulocytosis and positive direct Coombs test suggesting immune-mediated mechanism. Authors note DHA can occur after oral artemether-lumefantrine (not only IV artesunate) and recommend post-treatment hemoglobin monitoring (2–4 weeks) in high-risk patients
articles.title_original: Delayed hemolytic anemia after artemether-lumefantrine in Plasmodium falciparum malaria: A case report.

articles.headline: Meta-analysis of 10 studies (4,103 malaria cases, 2,460 controls) found the TIRAP/MAL rs8177374 T allele associated with reduced severe malaria risk (OR=0.51, 95% CI 0.40–0.65, p<0.001) but increased P. falciparum infection risk (OR=1.47, 95% CI 1.09–1.98, p=0.012), with no association for overall malaria susceptibility. TIRAP/MAL exerts a modulatory protective effect specifically against severe malaria pathogenesis
articles.title_original: Immunomodulatory effects of the TIRAP/MAL rs8177374 (S180L) variant: Meta-Analytic evidence from severe and Plasmodium falciparum malaria.

articles.headline: Scoping review of 10 Tanzanian studies (2015–2024) on 910 identified records found pyrethroid resistance widespread, driven by kdr mutations and metabolic mechanisms; DDT resistance common; moderate carbamate resistance in ~50% of studies. Organophosphates (pirimiphos-methyl) and newer insecticides (clothianidin, chlorfenapyr) showed high efficacy with no detected resistance, though surveillance gaps limit operational evidence
articles.title_original: Insecticide Resistance Management in Malaria Vectors in Tanzania Mainland (2015-2024): A Scoping Review.

articles.headline: Analysis of 52 bacterial infection and 73 malaria serum samples identified distinct immune signatures: bacterial acute febrile illness showed elevated endothelial activation (CD29+, CD62P+, CD9+ EVs) and MMP-2/MMP-9; malaria showed elevated antigen recognition (CD8+, CD81+, HLA-DR/DP/DQ+ EVs) and cytokine storm markers (IFNγ, CXCL10, TNFα). HLA-DR/DP/DQ+ EVs distinguished bacterial from malaria fever with AUC 0.87, supporting novel diagnostic markers
articles.title_original: Profiling of circulating immune factors and host-derived extracellular vesicles in response to bacterial or malaria acute fever.

articles.headline: Cross-sectional study of 452 children aged 6–23 months in Burkina Faso (25% clinical malaria, 88.5% anaemia) found adherence to a 'fat and vitamin A' dietary pattern inversely associated with clinical malaria (OR quintile 3 vs 1: 0.50, 95% CI 0.26–0.96) but not anaemia. Nutrient-rich diets may reduce clinical malaria risk in young children in endemic regions
articles.title_original: Associations Between Climate-Sensitive Nutrients, Clinical Malaria, and Anaemia Among Young Children in Rural Burkina Faso: An Analysis of Baseline Data From a Cluster-Randomised Controlled Trial.

articles.headline: Four RDT-positive, three microscopy-confirmed malaria cases in eastern Uttar Pradesh showed three with mixed P. falciparum/P. vivax infections and one P. falciparum monoinfection by PCR—a rare occurrence in the region. Cerebral malaria risk should be considered even in Pv and Pf co-infections, warranting closer government monitoring for the National Malaria Elimination Program
articles.title_original: Integrated diagnostic approach for mixed Plasmodium infection among acute febrile patients: rare incidence from Eastern Uttar Pradesh.

articles.headline: Cross-sectional study of 152 febrile patients (March–May 2024) at Buea Regional Hospital found malaria prevalence 52.0%, typhoid 37.5%, and co-infection in 22.3% of confirmed cases. All Salmonella typhi isolates were resistant to sulfometoxazole; vomiting was statistically associated with typhoid. Independent diagnosis and treatment are essential to reduce antimicrobial resistance and ensure proper care
articles.title_original: Malaria and salmonella typhi co- infection and antibiotic susceptibility in febrile patients at the Buea Regional Hospital.
articles.authors: Ebini-Ako TA, Tanih NF, Djakissam W

articles.headline: Pharmacokinetic analysis of data from two randomized controlled trials (2015–2020) comparing artemether-lumefantrine alone (n=443) versus plus amodiaquine (n=442) in uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria found no clinically relevant drug-drug interactions; geometric mean ratios for key parameters fell within the 0.80–1.25 range. Amodiaquine co-administration has minimal impact on artemether-lumefantrine pharmacokinetics
articles.title_original: Population pharmacokinetics of artemether-lumefantrine plus amodiaquine in patients with uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum malaria.

articles.headline: Historical analysis of 14,919 patient records from Vienna Psychiatric University Clinic (1951–1969) documents the application of malaria fever therapy, insulin coma therapy, cardiazol-induced convulsion therapy, and electroconvulsive therapy in treating neurosyphilis, schizophrenia, affective disorders, and intellectual disabilities during the transition to new pharmacological treatments. The study situates somatic therapies in the context of the emerging psychopharmacological revolution
articles.title_original: In the shadow of the "psychopharmacological revolution": Malaria fever, insulin coma, cardiazol and electroconvulsive therapy at the Vienna Psychiatric University Clinic, 1951-1969.

articles.headline: Phase 1 dose-escalation trial of MAM01 monoclonal antibody (targeting P. falciparum circumsporozoite NANP repeat region) in 37 adults (18–50 years) at doses 1.5–40 mg/kg IV or 5 mg/kg SC showed no treatment-related serious adverse events. After controlled human malaria infection with P. falciparum NF54, parasitaemia developed in 6/6 controls and 18/22 MAM01 recipients; 3/3 participants at 40 mg remained aparasitaemic (abstract incomplete)
articles.title_original: Human monoclonal antibody MAM01 for protection against malaria in adults in the USA: a first-in-human, phase 1, dose-escalation, double-blind, placebo-controlled, adaptive trial.

articles.headline: Title only: 'A new monoclonal in the arMAMentarium against malaria.' No abstract provided; unable to extract specific study design, intervention, or quantitative findings
articles.title_original: A new monoclonal in the arMAMentarium against malaria.

articles.headline: Title only: 'Postmalaria Labyrinthitis Ossificans: A Rare Complication of Plasmodium Infection.' No abstract provided; title suggests a case report or small case series documenting a rare otologic complication following Plasmodium infection
articles.title_original: Postmalaria Labyrinthitis Ossificans: A Rare Complication of Plasmodium Infection.

articles.headline: Review of erythrocyte invasion mechanisms in malaria pathogenesis and vaccine development found that blocking invasion-machinery components can prevent blood-stage malaria. Structural studies of antibodies induced by vaccination or natural infection revealed neutralizing epitopes and mechanisms of antibody potentiation; rational, structure-guided protein design leveraging these insights has produced the first rationally designed blood-stage malaria vaccine immunogen now entering clinical trials
articles.title_original: Erythrocyte invasion in malaria: from molecular mechanisms to rational vaccines.

articles.headline: Meta-analysis of 35 studies (9,503 neonates, 0-7 days) found pooled congenital malaria prevalence of 12.7% (95% CI: 7.3-19.2) in Nigeria with average parasitemia 56.6/μL. Maternal, placental, baby peripheral, and cord blood parasitemia rates were 25.5%, 20.3%, 12.9%, and 11.1%, respectively. Neonates with concurrent malaria and septicemia comprised 4.8% (3.4-6.7%), emphasizing significant congenital malaria burden requiring enhanced surveillance and targeted interventions
articles.title_original: Congenital malaria in Nigeria: A systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Odeniran PO, Paul-Odeniran KF, Ademola IO

articles.headline: Traction force microscopy of Plasmodium sporozoites showed TLP-lacking mutants generate lower overall forces than wild-type during gliding motility on substrate. TLP (thrombospondin-related anonymous protein-like) demonstrated importance as a component of the force-generating machinery essential for sporozoite migration speed and efficiency
articles.title_original: Traction force generation in Plasmodium sporozoites is modulated by a surface adhesin.

articles.headline: Retrospective cohort of 57 adults with severe imported P. falciparum malaria treated with intravenous artesunate showed 14.6% (6/41) had renal/neurological sequelae at day 28, associated with neurological failure (66.7% vs 14.3%, p=0.015), metabolic acidosis (50% vs 2.9%, p=0.007), and renal impairment (83.3% vs 2.9%, p<0.001). At remote follow-up, 18.2% (6/33) had sequelae; at one year, 57% (8/14) reported persistent fatigue and cognitive complaints
articles.title_original: Assessment of Long-Term Sequelae After Severe Malaria: A Retrospective Study.

articles.headline: Case series of five imported P. falciparum infections complicated by malaria-associated acute kidney injury (MAKI) in Tanzania showed mean serum creatinine 736 ± 346.4 μmol/L and urea 23.1 ± 14.1 mmol/L at admission. Four patients required hemodialysis with 100% survival, illustrating MAKI pathophysiology and modified natural history in low-endemic settings
articles.title_original: Malaria-Associated Acute Kidney Injury in a Low-Endemic Region: A Case Series of Five Imported Plasmodium falciparum Infections.

articles.headline: Systematic review of 25 studies (2,094 MAKI patients, 2000-2024) across endemic regions found P. falciparum most common but P. vivax increasingly causing severe AKI. Pooled MAKI incidence 26.5% (95% CI 19.8-34.4), rising to 77% in severe malaria; dialysis required 20-90% of cases; pooled mortality 19.2% (95% CI 12.9-27.7%). 5-20% developed chronic kidney disease, with thrombotic microangiopathy and acute cortical necrosis strongly associated with P. vivax
articles.title_original: Clinical characteristics and outcome of malaria-associated acute kidney injury in adult patients: a systematic review and meta analysis.

articles.headline: Genetic analysis of 72 P. falciparum clinical samples (2019-2021) from Ecuador and bordering Colombia using microsatellite markers and whole genome sequencing revealed shared, monoclonal parasites. A previously low-frequency Colombian clone caused new outbreaks through clonal expansion in Ecuador, demonstrating that low-prevalence clones can become epidemic and hamper elimination in low-transmission areas
articles.title_original: A previously low prevalence Plasmodium falciparum clone expands in an outbreak in the Pacific Coast of South America.

articles.headline: Chemoproteomics using amodiaquine (AQ)-derived activity probe and ABPP identified three glycolysis-associated enzymes as primary antimalarial targets of AQ in P. falciparum. AQ binds these proteins to disrupt glycolytic processes; complementary effects with artesunate (AS) on shared targets suggest synergism underlying AS-AQ combination therapy efficacy
articles.title_original: Chemoproteomics-based profiling elucidates the antimalarial effects of amodiaquine through disruption of glycolysis process in Plasmodium falciparum.

articles.headline: Evolutionary rate covariation (ERC) analysis across 22 Plasmodium species identified proteins with correlated evolutionary constraints indicating co-functional relationships. High ERC with known pathway proteins prioritizes unannotated genes; application identified PF3D7_0811600 co-localizing with rhoptry ion-channel proteins RhopH2 and RhopH3, enabling discovery of novel functional connections genome-wide
articles.title_original: Evolutionary rate covariation across malaria parasite species enables inference of protein interactions.

articles.headline: Longitudinal cohort of 1,071 individuals in pre-elimination southern Zambia (2018-2020) found 13% (144/1,071) positive for P. falciparum by qPCR at least once with median density 4 parasites/μL (monthly prevalence <4%). Clinical malaria incidence was 46.7 cases/1,000 person-years, but low-density asymptomatic parasitemia showed no association with subsequent clinical malaria or household transmission beyond one month
articles.title_original: Low-density asymptomatic parasitemia in southern Zambia does not lead to clinical malaria and is not associated with household transmission: results from a two-year cohort study.

articles.headline: Case report of 43-year-old male with severe P. falciparum cerebral malaria complicated by liver/kidney failure, cerebral edema, and heart failure treated with artemether and artificial liver blood purification combined with continuous renal replacement therapy resulted in successful recovery, suggesting combined extracorporeal support may rescue multi-organ failure
articles.title_original: Successful treatment of severe cerebral malaria with artificial liver blood purification technology: A case report.

articles.headline: Retrospective analysis of routine monthly data from 35 dispensaries across Niger (2018-2021) using SARIMA modeling found highly seasonal transmission peaking July-October. Children 12-59 months comprised 33.2% of cases, but children 10-14 years 26% and children 5-14 years together >33%, with southern agricultural districts reporting 3,000-7,500 monthly cases versus Niamey V peaking at 11,000, indicating need to expand SMC beyond children <5 years
articles.title_original: Burden of malaria among children aged 5-10 years in the Sahelian area: do we need to adapt seasonal malaria chemoprophylaxis?

articles.headline: Mesenchymal stem cell (MSC) therapy in P. berghei ANKA cerebral malaria mouse model reduced parasitemia, increased survival, prevented blood-brain barrier leakage, and modulated cytokines: downregulated IL-6 and IL-17, upregulated IL-10. MSCs uniquely induced RoRγt+Foxp3+ Tr17 regulatory cells that suppressed pathogenic Th17 response, suggesting potential adjunct therapy for cerebral malaria immunopathology
articles.title_original: Mesenchymal stem cells alleviate experimental cerebral malaria disease severity by inducing RoRγt(+) Foxp3(+) T regulatory (Tr 17) cells and modulating the dysregulated Th17/Treg axis.

articles.headline: In vitro and in vivo study of Feretia apodanthera extracts against Plasmodium berghei in mice showed non-toxic activity (LD50 >5000 mg/kg) with strong in vitro trophozoitocidal efficacy (IC50: 0.140–0.620 μg/mL). Leaf extract achieved superior chemosuppressive efficacy (68.01% at 250 mg/kg, 76.31% at 500 mg/kg), curative effects (68.41% Day 5, 63.41% Day 7), and prophylactic effects (63.60% Day 5, 57.10% Day 7). All organs exhibited antiplasmodial activity, with leaf demonstrating superior schizonticidal and chemosuppressive effects
articles.title_original: Harnessing the potential of medicinal plants in the malaria fighting: In vitro and in vivo antiplasmodial activities of Feretia apodanthera Delille (Rubiaceae).

articles.headline: Plasmodium yoelii infection in C57BL/6J mice with Lewis lung cancer inhibited tumor growth while increasing CD3+ T cells (including CD4+ and CD8+ subsets) in peripheral blood and tumor tissue. Infection reduced PD-1 expression on T cells, polymorphonuclear MDSCs, and immunosuppressive molecules pSTAT3 and TGFβ, while increasing M1/M2 macrophage ratio. Plasmodium infection remodels the immune system and inhibits tumor growth through reduction of pSTAT3 and TGFβ signaling, representing a distinct mechanism from checkpoint blockade
articles.title_original: Plasmodium infection fully activates the immune system in peripheral blood and tumor microenvironment in a murine Lewis lung cancer model.

articles.headline: Analysis of 202 Plasmodium falciparum CelTOS sequences from 201 patients in Jazan Province, Saudi Arabia revealed high haplotype diversity (Hd=0.921; 27 haplotypes) with low nucleotide diversity (π=0.0059), predominant purifying selection (gene-wide ω≈0.36), and low intragenic recombination (PHI p=1.44×10⁻⁵). Age (OR 1.03; 95% CI 1.00–1.06) and non-Saudi nationality (OR 2.21; 95% CI 1.02–4.81) were associated with very high parasitemia. High haplotype sharing across demographic strata in a largely panmictic population supports gene flow and validates CelTOS as a vaccine marker
articles.title_original: Genetic diversity and haplotype structure of the CelTOS gene in Plasmodium falciparum from Jazan Province, Saudi Arabia: implications for vaccine development and malaria elimination.

articles.headline: ELISA screening of Brazilian Amazon residents exposed to Plasmodium vivax found >60% with IgM or IgG antibodies against recombinant PvRipr protein, with predominant IgG1 and IgG2 responses. Four linear B-cell epitopes were identified, with B-PvRipr(879–888) and B-PvRipr(923–958) showing highest seropositive frequencies and strong correlation with IgG3 antibodies, a cytophilic subclass implicated in protective malaria immunity. PvRipr is naturally immunogenic with identified epitopes associated with protective antibody responses
articles.title_original: B-cell epitope mapping and characterization of antibody responses to recombinant PvRipr in malaria-exposed individuals.

articles.headline: An aluminum metasurface optical biosensor using surface plasmon resonance detected plasmodium falciparum lactate dehydrogenase (pfLDH) in phosphate-buffered saline with spectral sensitivity of 360 nm per RIU and limit of detection of 1.3 nM (45.6 ng/mL). The label-free visible-spectrum sensor provides a cost-effective, high-sensitivity method for active P. falciparum infection diagnosis
articles.title_original: Experimental plasmonic sensing of malaria using an aluminum metasurface.

articles.headline: Analysis of 118 Ghanaian children (cerebral malaria n=58, severe malarial anemia n=28, uncomplicated malaria n=32) found that PfHRP2-derived total parasite burden and sequestered burden were consistently higher than circulating burden in severe malaria syndromes. Cerebral malaria patients showed lower median circulating parasitemia than uncomplicated malaria despite greater parasite sequestration; after regaining consciousness, cerebral malaria patients exhibited decreased total and sequestered burden. PfHRP2-derived total parasite biomass demonstrated stronger association with severe malaria syndromes than peripheral parasitemia alone
articles.title_original: Reevaluating malaria severity metrics: insights from PfHRP2-derived biomass estimates in Ghanaian children.

articles.headline: Four-year surveillance (2020–2023) of Anopheles funestus in Cameroon documented sporozoite infection rates of 4–21% (predominantly Plasmodium falciparum) with dramatic pyrethroid resistance escalation (permethrin mortality declining from 77.7% in 2015 to 23.2% in 2023; deltamethrin from 46.6% to 8.5%). Full organophosphate susceptibility was maintained; rapid selection of G454A-CYP9K1 and 4.3 kb SV alleles occurred; PBO-based nets retained higher efficacy than pyrethroid-only nets. Intense pyrethroid resistance concurrent with high Plasmodium infection poses serious threat to malaria control
articles.title_original: Four-year monitoring of the malaria vector Anopheles funestus in central-west Cameroon reveals an escalation of pyrethroid resistance combined with high malaria transmission.

articles.headline: Human landing catch collections (July 2022–June 2023) in North Sumatra, Indonesia identified all 234 morphologically-suspected Anopheles maculatus specimens as A. maculatus s.s.; four (1.7%; 95% CI 0.5–4.3%) tested positive for Plasmodium DNA, with one specimen carrying both Plasmodium knowlesi and Plasmodium inui—the first detection of zoonotic parasite DNA in wild-caught A. maculatus. Anopheles maculatus is a potential vector of both zoonotic and human malarias in North Sumatra
articles.title_original: Evidence incriminating Anopheles maculatus as a potential vector of Plasmodium knowlesi and Plasmodium inui.

articles.headline: Cross-sectional survey of 1,603 pregnant women across Brazil, Ghana, Kenya, and Pakistan regarding attitudes toward TB, malaria, GBS, and RSV vaccines found vaccine safety for the baby was the primary decision factor. Regarding vaccine acceptance, 30% would definitely receive GBS vaccine, 26% malaria, 25% RSV, and 19% TB; approximately 40% agreed vaccines are unnatural and 38% believed the body should develop natural immunity. Pregnant women demonstrate interest in vaccination despite vaccine hesitancy concerns
articles.title_original: Pregnant women's attitudes and intentions toward tuberculosis, malaria, group B streptococcus, and respiratory syncytial virus vaccines in pregnant: Findings from a cross-sectional study of pregnant women living in Brazil, Ghana, Kenya, and Pakistan.

articles.headline: Secondary analysis of 1,019 malnourished children from Burkina Faso randomized to 12 weeks supplementation (500 kcal/d) with corn-soy blend or lipid-based supplements found iron deficiency associated with 82% (95% CI 76–87) lower serum hepcidin, symptomatic malaria with 103% (95% CI 32–210) higher hepcidin, and asymptomatic/recent malaria with 51% (95% CI 35–63) lower hepcidin. Supplementation increased hepcidin 61% (95% CI 38–87) overall, with 22% higher concentration in lipid-based supplements versus corn-soy blend. Decreased hepcidin characterizes malnourished children with asymptomatic malaria
articles.title_original: Hepcidin is low in children with moderate acute malnutrition and asymptomatic malaria: secondary analysis of a 2×2×3 factorial randomised trial in Burkina Faso.

articles.headline: Western blotting and surface plasmon resonance analysis of PfRipr5 (a truncated Plasmodium falciparum Rh5-interacting protein fragment containing EGF-like domains 5–9) identified a 20-amino-acid cysteine-associated epitope recognized by growth-inhibitory monoclonal antibody 29B11. Epitope recognition is dependent on cysteine integrity, providing a molecular entry point for dissecting PfRipr function. This growth-inhibitory epitope supports epitope-focused rational design of subunit vaccines against blood-stage P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Identification of a growth-inhibitory epitope in PfRipr5, a malaria vaccine candidate against Plasmodium falciparum.

articles.headline: Epidemiologic surveillance with P. falciparum genomics (MAD4HatTeR panel: 165 diversity markers, 38 drug resistance loci) from three southwestern Uganda health facilities (January 2023–June 2024) revealed recent overnight travel was common in very low transmission areas (Maziba 87%, Muko 96%) but infrequent in low-to-moderate transmission Kamwezi (12%). Transmission network inference identified most Maziba/Muko infections as imported while Kamwezi showed sustained local transmission; artemisinin partial resistance markers (K13 P441L, R561H) were more prevalent in Kamwezi. Tailored interventions addressing travel-associated importation and local transmission are needed for elimination
articles.title_original: Imported malaria predominates in near-elimination settings in Southwestern Uganda.

articles.headline: Pharmacophore modeling and molecular docking identified natural product inhibitors against mutant P. falciparum DHFR using validated hypotheses (r=0.949, r²=0.883) from training set of 28 compounds. Top candidate ZINC70454408 showed highest H-bond energy (-14.204 kcal/mol), predicted Ki of 0.04 µM, and stable RMSD of 2.1 Å in 200 ns molecular dynamics simulations. Two additional compounds (ZINC04096650, ZINC85631105) exhibited strong binding energies (-122.64 and -118.92 kcal/mol) with Ki values <0.1 µM
articles.title_original: Computational exploration of natural product inhibitors against mutant Plasmodium falciparum DHFR through pharmacophore modeling, docking, and MD simulations.

articles.headline: WHO insecticide susceptibility tests in three urban Nigerian centers (Awka, Onitsha, Nnewi) revealed An. coluzzii populations with resistance to pyrethroids and DDT but susceptibility to carbamates and organophosphates. Deltamethrin mortality was low (45-63%) and DDT showed markedly reduced susceptibility (20-56%), while bendiocarb and pirimiphos-methyl maintained 100% efficacy. High kdr L1014F allele frequencies (0.72-0.81) and low ace-1 G119S frequencies (0.07-0.12) explain resistance patterns
articles.title_original: Urban evolution of insecticide resistance and susceptibility patterns of Anopheles coluzzii in Southeastern Nigeria: implications for malaria vector control.

articles.headline: Participatory photovoice study with 20 participants from three rural Sabah villages documented experiences with P. knowlesi (monkey malaria) over 20 days (January-May 2024) using smartphone photography and focus group discussions. Analysis generated five themes including monkey malaria as embedded everyday risk, community resilience through locally derived practices, and cultural knowledge with blended coping strategies. Findings emphasize need for culturally appropriate interventions addressing local priorities and lived experience rather than strictly biomedical approaches
articles.title_original: What does monkey malaria mean to you? A participatory photovoice study in rural Malaysian Borneo.

articles.headline: Vector-host compartmental mathematical model compared three approaches to incorporating temperature and rainfall effects on malaria transmission dynamics at varying vector control efficacy levels. Different modeling approaches projected different transmission patterns: sustained seasonal transmission, extended seasonal peaks in tropical/semi-arid regions, or multiple irregular peaks with winter cessation. Results demonstrate sustained and effective vector control can substantially reduce malaria incidence, though climatic events like heatwaves or flooding can trigger outbreaks even in controlled areas
articles.title_original: Comparing three approaches to modelling the effects of temperature and rainfall on malaria incidence in different climatic regions.
articles.authors: Madito GT, Brooke BD, Silal SP

articles.headline: Cross-sectional reactive case detection (July-October 2022) screened 2,009 individuals following 142 index cases using microscopy and nested PCR. PCR-corrected microscopy-based prevalence was 3.6% (72/2009; 95% CI 2.8-4.5) with submicroscopic infections at 10.1% (33/326; 95% CI 7.2-13.9), comprising 31% of total PCR-confirmed cases. P. vivax gametocyte carriage was 69% versus 22% for P. falciparum, and index cases showed higher asexual parasite density (16,177 vs. 1,900/μL; P<0.001) but lower gametocyte carriage than reactive cases
articles.title_original: Community reservoirs of malaria parasites and gametocytes in Arba Minch district, southern Rift Valley, Ethiopia: a cross-sectional study.

articles.headline: Study identified nanobodies against Pfs48/45 on P. falciparum sexual-stage parasites that recognize gametocytes with strong transmission-reducing activity. Crystal structure of the most potent nanobody revealed binding to a distinct epitope from TB31F monoclonal antibody, though similar to RUPA-44 and RUPA-117. These findings demonstrate the potential of nanobodies as a versatile antibody format for malaria transmission reduction
articles.title_original: Pfs48/45 nanobodies block Plasmodium falciparum transmission.

articles.headline: Conditional mutagenesis study showed P. berghei DNA ligase I (PbLig1) is located primarily in the nucleus throughout the parasite lifecycle. Knockout of PbLig1 in sporozoites revealed it is essential for liver-stage development, as parasites lacking the enzyme invade hepatocytes but arrest during mid-liver stage with limited nuclear division and reduced DNA content. These findings demonstrate PbLig1 is essential for parasite blood- and liver-stage development
articles.title_original: Plasmodium DNA ligase I is essential for parasite blood- and liver-stage development.

articles.headline: Narrative review synthesized current and emerging malaria prevention strategies including vector control (insecticide-treated nets, indoor residual spraying), chemoprevention (intermittent preventive treatment in pregnancy, seasonal malaria chemoprevention), and immunoprophylaxis (RTS,S/AS01 and R21/Matrix-M vaccines). Recent vaccine rollouts demonstrated reductions in severe disease and mortality. Emerging approaches such as monoclonal antibodies and genetically modified mosquitoes offer promise, though challenges remain in ensuring equitable access and sustaining efficacy amid widespread insecticide and drug resistance
articles.title_original: The Evolving Landscape of Malaria Prevention Strategies: A Review of Recent Developments.

articles.headline: Proximity-dependent biotin identification (UltraID) combined with genome editing identified sporozoite-specific rhoptry proteins in P. berghei using RON12 as bait. Study identified novel rhoptry molecule PBANKA_1363400 localized to sporozoite rhoptries and predominantly expressed in sporozoites rather than merozoites. UltraID strategy successfully biotinylated several secretory proteins during parasite maturation in mosquitoes, enabling sensitive species- and stage-specific protein identification
articles.title_original: Identification of a novel rhoptry protein expressed predominantly in Plasmodium sporozoites.

articles.headline: In vitro enzyme inhibition study evaluated potato carboxypeptidase inhibitor (PCI) against carboxypeptidase-B1 from Anopheles stephensi (CPBAs1). PCI efficiently inhibited CPBAs1 enzymatic activity at low concentrations, with inhibition comparable to commercial 1,10-phenanthroline inhibitor. Findings support PCI's potential as effector molecule for paratransgenesis-based malaria vector control
articles.title_original: Inhibition of carboxypeptidase b1 from Anopheles stephensi by the potato carboxypeptidase inhibitor: a foundational step toward paratransgenesis-based malaria control.

articles.headline: Comparative study of PTRAMP, CSS, and Ripr orthologs from P. falciparum, P. vivax, and P. knowlesi demonstrated that PTRAMP and CSS form disulfide-linked heterodimers and complex with Ripr (PCR complex) across all three species. Cross-reactive antibodies differentially inhibited merozoite invasion. Cryo-EM of P. knowlesi PCR complex confirmed a core invasion scaffold conserved across Plasmodium species with implications for vaccines targeting multiple malaria species
articles.title_original: PTRAMP, CSS and Ripr form a conserved complex required for merozoite invasion of Plasmodium species into erythrocytes.

articles.headline: Negative-binomial generalized additive models analyzed 132 monthly observations (2013-2023) of malaria incidence, severe cases, and deaths in Tarkwa-Nsuaem, Ghana. Monthly incidence median was 847 cases; rainfall "memory" improved model (AIC 1,269→1,234; deviance explained ~80%; adjusted R²~0.88) with significant 1-month rainfall lag and 6-month accumulation effects. Twelve-month forecast projected ~600-670 monthly incidence cases (95% PI ~250-1,350) and ~0-1 deaths per month
articles.title_original: Malaria incidence, severity and mortality in children under five in Ghana: evidence from generalised additive models.
articles.authors: Bosson-Amedenu S, Eyiah-Bediako F, Anafo AY

articles.headline: Young West African male presented with pyogenic splenic abscess concurrent with P. falciparum malaria and sickle cell trait; cultures identified Fusobacterium species. Treatment with antimalarial therapy and broad-spectrum antibiotics with anaerobic coverage resulted in complete recovery after percutaneous drainage. Case demonstrates importance of considering anaerobic bacterial pathogens in splenic abscesses among malaria-endemic populations
articles.title_original: Pyogenic Splenic Abscess With Concurrent Active Plasmodium falciparum Malaria and Sickle Cell Trait: A Case Report.

articles.headline: 66-year-old woman with P. falciparum cerebral malaria developed purpura fulminans with digital gangrene despite early intravenous artesunate, hyperbaric oxygen therapy, and supportive care. Systemic recovery was achieved but gangrene persisted, illustrating the severe microvascular thrombosis complications of malaria
articles.title_original: Purpura Fulminans With Digital Gangrene in Severe Plasmodium falciparum Malaria: A Case Presentation.

articles.headline: Among 3,488 Ugandan women exposed to malaria prevention messages, community health workers (ATT=0.322) and community events (ATT=0.296) significantly increased bed net use, followed by social mobilization (ATT=0.185). Television exposure (ATT=0.051) showed no significant effect. Overall 73.2% reported bed net use the previous night, with community-based channels outperforming mass media for behavior change
articles.title_original: Differentiated effects of sources of malaria messages on mosquito bed net use among women of reproductive age in Uganda: a propensity score matched analysis.

articles.headline: In a murine P. yoelii model, malaria-induced intestinal barrier loss enabled microbiota-derived purinergic suppression characterized by systemic adenosine persistence and CD39+ plasmablast expansion. Therapeutic gut barrier reinforcement or adenosine A2A receptor blockade improved germinal centers and reduced disease severity independent of parasite burden, revealing a microbiota-mediated immunosuppression pathway in severe malaria
articles.title_original: Intestinal Barrier Loss Enables Microbiota-Mediated Purinergic Suppression During Malaria.

articles.headline: Cross-sectional survey of 768 Pakistan residents found 24.5% malaria prevalence. Independent risk factors included male gender (AOR 1.8), ages 16-30 (AOR 4.2), 31-50 (AOR 8.3), and >50 (AOR 3.6), low education (AOR 3.9), low income (AOR 17.8), large family size (AOR 11.3), distance >3 km to healthcare (AOR 3.1), and lack of modern toilets (AOR 4.8)
articles.title_original: Determinants of malaria infection across different districts of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, Pakistan: a cross-sectional study.

articles.headline: Seven cases of long-latency P. malariae in Guangdong (2014–2022) showed female predominance (85.7%), mean age 75.3 years, with latency periods of 7–50 years. Infection origins were local transmission (6 cases) or Southeast Asian importation (1 case); phylogenetic analysis revealed Asian lineage with 95%–97% nucleotide homology, distinct from African strains (92%–95%). Median diagnostic delay was 4 days
articles.title_original: Unraveling the origins and features of long-latency Plasmodium malariae infections: a comprehensive epidemiological, clinical, and genetic study of seven cases in Guangdong Province (2014-2022).

articles.headline: A stabilized tandem antigen chimera (STAC) vaccine presenting Pfs230-D1 and Pfs48/45-D3 epitopes elicited antibodies with potent transmission-reducing activity in female mice comparable or superior to individually multimerized antigens. STAC represents a promising next-generation transmission-blocking vaccine candidate for P. falciparum, demonstrating the utility of engineered multi-domain antigens
articles.title_original: A stabilized tandem antigen chimera that elicits potent malaria transmission-reducing activity.

articles.headline: Among 574 caregivers in northern Uganda, 90.1% (95% CI 87.3–92.4) were aware of the R21 malaria vaccine, but only 13.9% (95% CI 11.2–17.0) demonstrated factual knowledge (doses 16.4%, intervals 21.0%, age eligibility 34.2%). While 57.4% expressed positive attitudes and 57.5% positive perceptions, roughly half reported safety concerns, indicating need for targeted community education campaigns
articles.title_original: Caregivers awareness, knowledge, attitudes, and perceptions towards malaria vaccine in Kole and Kwania Districts, Northern Uganda.

articles.headline: Retrospective analysis of 100,760 microscopy-tested patients over 5 years (2020–2024) at a hospital in Southwest Ethiopia identified 14,303 (14.2%) with malaria: P. vivax 49.1%, P. falciparum 37.8%, mixed infections 13.1%. Positivity rate increased from 9.2% (2020) to 17.7% (2024), with females representing 51.4% of suspected and 51.8% of confirmed cases
articles.title_original: A five-year (2020-2024) malaria surveillance data analysis of Mattu Karl Specialized Comprehensive Hospital, Oromia region, Southwest Ethiopia.

articles.headline: Historical perspective examining malaria's overlooked role in early Arab-Israeli conflict and the origins of Palestinian malaria control efforts over 100 years ago. This paper discusses how malaria's severity rendered much of rural Palestine nearly uninhabitable in the pre-Zionist era, though specific epidemiological data are not provided
articles.title_original: Malaria in Palestine: the elephant in the room.

articles.headline: Malaria-MOI, a Python tool for estimating multiplicity of infection (MOI) in P. falciparum from whole-genome sequencing data, achieved RMSE 0.38, MAE 0.15, and correlation 0.78 when benchmarked on 27 mixed-clone samples. Applied to 8,208 field samples, it showed strong negative correlation with FWS (ρ=−0.76), high sensitivity for monoclonal infections (0.99), and superior specificity (0.76) compared to estMOI, accurately capturing transmission intensity
articles.title_original: Malaria-MOI: A flexible and scalable tool for predicting multiplicity of infection in malaria parasites.

articles.headline: Cross-sectional survey of 1,672 participants in Côte d'Ivoire found 41.1% malaria prevalence, with LLIN ownership 66.3% but usage only 50% in 5–9 year-olds, 44.3% in 10–14 years, and 56.9% in ≥15 years versus 63.2% in under-fives. Under-fives had 61.3% prevalence; 5–9 years had highest risk at 75.0% (aOR 1.86). LLIN users showed 38.5% prevalence (aOR 0.74) versus 44.3% in non-users, with marked disparities by gender and wealth
articles.title_original: Disparities in long-lasting insecticidal bed net usage and malaria burden 2 years after a mass distribution campaign in central Côte d'Ivoire: A cross-sectional survey prior to a cluster randomised trial.

articles.headline: Expert consensus study using Nominal Group Technique (NGT) with malaria program implementers identified 20 key factors influencing P. knowlesi prevention behaviors across four themes (environmental, human-related, policy/organizational, and vector risks) with 100% voting response and single-round 80% consensus achievement, providing a foundation for tailored prevention strategies in at-risk Southeast Asian communities
articles.title_original: Exploring influencing factors affecting preventive behaviours of Plasmodium knowlesi malaria among at-risk communities: an application of the nominal group technique.

articles.headline: Nanobodies targeting domain 3 of P. falciparum HAP2 fusogen demonstrated nanomolar-affinity binding with cross-species reactivity to other Plasmodium HAP2 orthologs; crystal structure of one nanobody-domain 3 complex provides first structural insights into this transmission-blocking target for potential vaccine development
articles.title_original: Crystal structure and nanobodies against domain 3 of the malaria parasite fusogen Plasmodium falciparum HAP2.

articles.headline: Compound 5ac, a γ-tetrahydrocarboline-derived PfHDAC1 inhibitor, demonstrated potent P. falciparum activity (Pf3D7 IC50=4.1 nM, selectivity index 358 over HepG2, 3573 over HEK293T), improved physicochemical properties, and potent in vivo antimalarial efficacy; mechanistic studies showed downregulation of invasion-related genes, establishing this scaffold as a promising drug candidate against artemisinin-resistant parasites
articles.title_original: Discovery of γ-Tetrahydrocarboline Derivatives as Plasmodium falciparum Histone Deacetylase Inhibitors for Treatment of Malaria.

articles.headline: Proteomic profiling of microparticles from 43 malaria patients versus 10 controls identified 1729 proteins in malaria-derived microparticles (versus 234 in controls), with significantly elevated hemoglobin subunits in malaria samples and culture supernatant, suggesting microparticle-mediated hemoglobin loss as a mechanism initiating anemia in P. falciparum infection
articles.title_original: Integrative proteomic profiling of malaria-derived microparticles: A mass spectrometry-based study.

articles.headline: Comprehensive review of mosquito-borne diseases accounting for 17% of global infectious disease and ~700,000 annual deaths, covering dengue, Zika, chikungunya, malaria, and lymphatic filariasis; summarizes vector-borne transmission dynamics, control strategies including R21 malaria vaccine and novel vector management approaches, and socioeconomic burden (~$318 billion for Aedes-borne diseases since 1975)
articles.title_original: Review of selected mosquito-borne diseases: arboviruses (dengue, chikungunya, Zika, West Nile, Japanese encephalitis, yellow fever) and parasitic diseases (malaria, lymphatic Filariasis).

articles.headline: Meta-analysis of 15 studies (2018-2025) across Africa, Asia, and South America found global asymptomatic malaria prevalence among blood donors of 0.32% (95% CI: 0.25-0.39), with substantial regional variation: Africa 15.89%, South America 0.79%, Asia 0.21%; diagnostic method significantly affected detection (microscopy 21.86%, rapid diagnostic test 0.21%, PCR 5.03%), highlighting critical transfusion safety risks and need for region-specific screening strategies
articles.title_original: Global burden of asymptomatic malaria among blood donors: systematic review and meta-analysis.
